section.title: Overview
section.para: The rust_exec() function lets you write &sip; processing logic in Rust and load it at runtime, without rebuilding the Rust module. It works exactly like python_exec() and lua_exec() — you point the module at a shared library, and call exported functions from your &osips; routes.
Both approaches coexist in the same module:

row.entry: Functions like rust_check_rate() built into the module

section.title: 1. Create a new crate
section.programlisting: cargo new --lib my-opensips-handler cd my-opensips-handler

section.title: 2. Configure Cargo.toml
section.para: The cdylib crate type produces a .so file suitable for dlopen. Add external crates as needed — for example, the included example handlers add serde_json = "1" for JSON parsing.

section.title: 3. Write handlers
4. Build
section.programlisting: cargo build --release # Output: target/release/libmy_opensips_handler.so

section.title: 5. Configure &osips;
ABI Contract
section.para: User scripts export extern "C" functions with this signature:
section.programlisting: extern "C" fn(msg: *mut c_void, param: *const c_char, param_len: c_int) -> c_int
section.para: The opensips_handler! macro hides this ABI and gives you:

listitem.para: msg: a SipMessage with safe accessors
param: an Option<&str>

section.title: Example Handlers Reference
section.para: The modules/rust/examples/user-script/src/lib.rs file contains 19 self-contained handlers, each one a teaching lesson that maps Rust concepts to &sip; processing patterns.

row.entry: if let Some(...), msg.set_pv(), early returns
bidirectional PV exchange, splitn(), string transforms
persistent per-worker state, u64 counter, PV output
cross-worker atomic counter via SharedAtomicCounter in shm
pv() vs pv_get(), set_pv() vs set_pv_int(), PvValue enum
null/deleted AVPs, empty strings, read-only PVs, boundary ints
raw TCP HTTP/1.0, TcpStream, Duration timeout, Result chaining
opensips_async_handler!, AsyncContext, non-blocking I/O, resume callbacks

section.title: 1. hello_world
section.para: Minimal handler — log the &sip; method and return success. Introduces the opensips_handler! macro, one-argument closures, and Option::unwrap_or() for default values.

section.title: 2. greet
section.para: Demonstrates the optional parameter passed from opensips.cfg. The two-argument closure |msg, param| receives param as Option<&str> — Rust's null alternative that the compiler forces you to handle. Uses unwrap_or() for a default greeting and msg.source_ip() for caller identification.

section.title: 3. log_user_agent
section.para: Reads and logs the User-Agent &sip; header using msg.header(), which returns Option<&str> with case-insensitive lookup. The _param underscore prefix tells the Rust compiler the parameter is intentionally unused.

section.title: 4. append_custom_header
section.para: Adds an X-Rust-Processed header to the &sip; message using msg.call("append_hf", ...). Teaches cross-module dispatch via msg.call(), the difference between Result<T,E> and Option<T>, and &sip; header \r\n line ending requirements.
Requires: loadmodule "textops.so" in your config.

section.title: 5. route_by_header
section.para: Reads an X-Route-To custom header and sets $du (destination URI) accordingly. Demonstrates if let Some(...) pattern matching, PV writes with msg.set_pv(), and the early-return style preferred in Rust.

section.title: 6. caller_screening
section.para: Blocks calls from a hardcoded IP blacklist using thread_local! { HashSet }. This is the canonical pattern for per-worker state in &osips; Rust modules. Explains why thread_local! is safe (fork model), what RefCell does (interior mutability), and why HashSet provides O(1) lookups.

section.title: 7. number_portability
section.para: LNP (Local Number Portability) cache with TTL expiration. Maps dialed numbers to carrier routing destinations using thread_local! { HashMap }. Teaches Rust struct definitions, Instant + Duration for monotonic time, and the HashMap::entry() API that avoids double lookups.

section.title: 8. request_logger
section.para: CDR-like structured logging that dumps call metadata (Call-ID, From, To, method) and iterates all &sip; headers. Teaches msg.header_iter() which returns an Iterator<Item = (&str, &str)>, tuple destructuring, and PV reads via msg.pv().

section.title: 9. rate_limit_by_ua
section.para: Rate limiter keyed by User-Agent header instead of source IP. Uses the same thread_local! + RefCell + HashMap pattern as the compiled-in rust_check_rate(). Demonstrates .parse::<u32>() for string-to-number conversion and chained .unwrap_or() for fallback defaults.

section.title: 10. set_routing_flags
section.para: Sets &osips; message flags and $var() pseudo-variables for downstream routing decisions. The config script can then check isflag(1) or $var(call_type). Teaches msg.flags(), msg.set_flag(), starts_with() string matching, and writing multiple PVs in sequence.

section.title: 11. reply_handler
section.para: Classifies &sip; replies by status code range (1xx provisional, 2xx success, etc.) using Rust's match with range patterns (200..=299). Best used in onreply_route or failure_route. Teaches msg.is_reply(), msg.status_code(), and the exhaustive match pattern.

section.title: 12. variable_exchange
section.para: Bidirectional PV exchange — the key pattern for config-to-Rust communication. The config sets $var(input) before calling rust_exec("variable_exchange"), Rust reads it, processes the command (uppercase/reverse/len/prefix), and writes $var(output) + $var(rc) back so the config can branch on the results.
Teaches msg.pv() for reading config-set variables, msg.set_pv() for writing results back, splitn() for parsing, and match on string slices for dispatch.

section.title: 13. call_counter
section.para: Persistent per-worker request counter using thread_local! { HashMap<String, u64> }. The simplest demonstration of state that survives across &sip; transactions. Writes $var(count) and $var(counter_name) back to the config.
section.programlisting: rust_exec("call_counter", "invites"); xlog("counter: $var(counter_name) = $var(count)\n");
section.para: Teaches named counters via the optional param, entry().or_insert(0) for default initialization, and u64::to_string() for numeric-to-PV conversion.

section.title: 14. shared_counter
section.para: Truly atomic cross-worker counter using SharedAtomicCounter (SDK type that wraps AtomicI64 in &osips; shared memory). Unlike $shv() which has a read-modify-write race condition, this uses fetch_add — a single CPU instruction (lock xadd on x86-64) that is truly atomic across all workers.

The compiled-in rust_counter_inc() allocates the AtomicI64 in shared memory during mod_init (before fork). After fork, all workers share the same physical counter. The user-script handler calls it via msg.call("rust_counter_inc", &[]).
Teaches the difference between per-worker state (thread_local!), non-atomic shared state ($shv()), and truly atomic shared state (SharedAtomicCounter).

section.title: 15. pv_manipulation
section.para: Comprehensive pseudo-variable manipulation — reads core &sip; PVs ($rm, $ru, $rU, $rd, $si, $sp), writes string PVs via set_pv(), writes integer PVs via set_pv_int(), and inspects PV types via pv_get() returning the PvValue enum (Int/Str/Null).

Teaches the three ways to read PVs (pv() format strings, pv_get() raw values, header() direct access), the two ways to write PVs (set_pv() for strings, set_pv_int() for integers), and the PvValue enum for type-safe value inspection.

section.title: 16. avp_operations
section.para: AVP (Attribute-Value Pair) operations — the critical mechanism for passing data across &sip; transaction boundaries. Unlike $var() which dies when the route ends, $avp() values survive t_relay() and are readable in failure_route and onreply_route.

Demonstrates string AVPs ($avp(rust_caller_ip), $avp(rust_call_type)), integer AVPs ($avp(rust_timestamp) via set_pv_int()), and AVP stacking (multiple writes to $avp(rust_tag) create a LIFO stack). The optional param controls the top-of-stack tag value.
Teaches the critical difference between $var() (route-scoped) and $avp() (transaction-scoped), when to use each, and AVP stacking semantics.

section.title: 17. pv_edge_cases
section.para: Robustness test — the config intentionally feeds bad/missing data to Rust: deleted AVPs ($null), never-set PVs, empty strings, near-limit 3800-byte strings, i32::MAX and -1 boundary integers, writes to read-only PVs ($ci), and malformed PV specs. Every case must return None/Err gracefully without crashing &osips;.

After deletion, writes "resurrected" back to $avp(edge_test) to prove AVPs can be re-created after $null deletion. Reports pass/fail count via $var(edge_checks) and overall result via $var(edge_result).
Teaches defensive programming with Rust's type system: Option::is_none(), Result::is_err(), PvValue::Null matching, and why testing failure paths is as important as testing success paths.

section.title: 18. http_query
section.para: Raw &http; client using only std — no external dependencies (no ureq, no reqwest). Uses std::net::TcpStream with &http;/1.0 (server closes connection, giving simple EOF detection) and a 2-second timeout to prevent worker blocking.

Writes $var(http_status), $var(http_body), $var(http_time_ms), and $var(http_error). The helper simple_http_get() demonstrates URL parsing with strip_prefix/split_once, Result chaining with map_err, and Duration-based timeouts on TcpStream. Only supports http:// (no TLS in std).

Teaches the tradeoff between blocking I/O in a &sip; worker (simple but blocks the worker for the duration of the &http; call) vs async patterns (complex but non-blocking). For production use, prefer the compiled-in http_pool module which uses a dedicated thread pool.

section.title: 19. json_parse
section.para: JSON parsing with serde_json — the standard Rust JSON library. Two modes: config-provided JSON via $var(json_input), or auto-built JSON from &sip; message fields (method, source IP, source port).

Writes $var(json_parsed) ("ok"/"error"), $var(json_method), $var(json_src_ip), $var(json_fields) (comma-separated top-level keys), $var(json_raw) (echo of parsed JSON), and $var(json_src_port) (integer).

Teaches serde_json::from_str returning Result<Value, Error>, dynamic field access via value["key"].as_str(), numeric extraction via as_u64(), iterating object keys via as_object(), and the json! macro for building JSON programmatically. Handles malformed JSON gracefully by writing error details to $var(json_error).

section.title: 20. async_http_query
section.para: Non-blocking &http; query using &osips; async() framework — the key pattern for calling external services without blocking &sip; workers. Unlike handler 18 (http_query) which blocks the worker for the entire &http; call duration, this handler:

listitem.para: Opens a TCP connection and sends the request
Hands the fd to the &osips; reactor
Returns immediately — the worker processes other &sip; traffic
When the response arrives, the reactor calls the resume callback
The resume callback reads the response and writes PVs/AVPs
The resume route runs with the populated data

section.para: For the full list of output pseudo-variables and error conditions, see .

Teaches opensips_async_handler! macro, AsyncContext methods (set_fd, set_resume, set_resume_param, set_timeout, done_sync), Box::into_raw()/Box::from_raw() for cross-FFI state transfer, and AVP stacking for list-like data.

section.title: How &osips; async() works
section.para: The async() script statement suspends the current &sip; transaction, monitors a file descriptor for I/O readiness, then resumes a route when data arrives. This is how modules like rest_client avoid blocking workers during &http; calls.
section.programlisting: # Config syntax: async(rust_exec("handler_name", "param"), resume_route_name);

section.title: Writing async handlers
section.para: Use the opensips_async_handler! macro instead of opensips_handler!:
Handler naming convention: The function name MUST be prefixed with async_. For full details on async handler resolution and fallback behavior, see .

section.title: Async ABI contract
section.para: Async handlers export this signature (generated by opensips_async_handler!):
section.programlisting: extern "C" fn(msg: *mut c_void, ctx: *mut c_void, param: *const c_char, param_len: c_int) -> c_int

row.entry: Opaque pointer to async_ctx (wrapped as AsyncContext)

section.title: Resume callbacks
section.para: The resume function is called by the &osips; reactor when the monitored fd becomes readable:
Return values:

listitem.para: ASYNC_DONE (-1): async complete, continue to resume route
ASYNC_DONE_CLOSE_FD (-2): complete + close fd
ASYNC_CONTINUE (-5): not done yet, keep monitoring

section.title: Memory model
section.para: Since async() (script-level) resumes in the same worker process, regular Rust heap allocation (Box) works for resume state. Use Box::into_raw() to transfer ownership to the raw pointer, and Box::from_raw() in the resume callback to recover it. Rust's ownership system ensures exactly one free.
For async_launch() (fire-and-forget where a different process may resume), you would need shm_malloc instead.

listitem.para: Panic isolation: If your handler panics, the panic is caught at two boundaries (your function + the module dispatcher). &osips; continues running and the route returns -1.
Symbol isolation: Your .so is loaded with RTLD_LOCAL, so its symbols do not leak into the &osips; global namespace or conflict with other modules.
Fail-fast loading: RTLD_NOW ensures all symbols are resolved at load time. If your .so has missing dependencies, &osips; will fail at startup with a clear error, not at runtime.
Function caching: After the first dlsym lookup, function pointers are cached in a HashMap. Subsequent calls to the same function avoid repeated symbol resolution.

section.title: "dlopen failed: cannot open shared object file"

listitem.para: Check the path in script_name is absolute and readable by the &osips; user.
Verify the .so exists: ls -la /path/to/libmy_handler.so
Check library dependencies: ldd /path/to/libmy_handler.so
Ensure you used opensips_handler! (which applies #[no_mangle]).
Check exported symbols: nm -D /path/to/libmy_handler.so | grep your_function
Verify crate-type is cdylib in your Cargo.toml.
Add modparam("rust", "script_name", "/path/to/lib.so") to your config.
The modparam must appear before the route block.
Check &osips; logs for "panic in function_name(): ..." messages.
The panic message includes the Rust panic payload for debugging.
The route continues with return value -1.
Your script crate must use the same opensips-rs SDK version as the module.
Rebuild both the module and your script when upgrading &osips;.
